sedation services, and trained gastroenterologists. VCE, although less invasive, is costly and comes with a 
small but definite risk of capsule retention.(139-141) Unfortunately, no formal studies have systematically 
assessed the issues related to access to the instruments and trained personnel for small-bowel endoscopy 
in LMICs. In a questionnaire survey in Brazil, almost three-quarters of physicians reported difficulty in 
conducting small-bowel examinations [DAE and VCE].(142) A relatively old publication suggested that 
there were delays in the provision of endoscopy, a lack of equipment and endoscope maintenance, issues 
with disinfection, and a lack of trained human resources in one of the regions of South Africa, a relatively 
more developed region of Africa.(143)   
Statement 9b 
The practices and availability of sedation during endoscopy in LMICs are variable. Sedation 
might be a preferable option to encourage increased and better endoscopic examinations, and 
it should be guided by patient preferences, pre-endoscopic assessment, and local resources 
[EL5].
Supporting text
Sedation during gastrointestinal endoscopy is a well-established practice with a focus on patient safety 
and comfort. The choice of sedative agents and the level of sedation (deep sedation, moderate [conscious] 
sedation, or procedures without sedation) should be individualized, considering patient-specific factors 
and procedural requirements.(144) However, even in developed countries there is no consensus on the 
type and depth of sedation. Arguments against sedation include cost-effectiveness, safety, patient 
turnaround, and patient preference.(145-147) There is a significant gap in knowledge in LMICs regarding 
sedation practices. Sedation availability is variable in LMICs.(148) There is also variability in access to 
anaesthetist- or nurse-driven sedation practices in endoscopy. Offering sedation often requires the 
availability of anaesthesiologist support in case of complications or delayed recovery from anaesthesia. 
Patient awareness and fear of the procedure in general and sedation itself are also significant issues that 
may impact patient willingness to undergo a procedure, which may lead to delayed or missed diagnosis. 
Updated surveys on patient awareness and preferences are advised.

IBD IN LMICS
Statement 10
There are several infectious diseases that can mimic IBD, and histological features may not be 
pathognomonic. Granulomas, although suggestive, are not diagnostic of CD and have numerous 
differential diagnoses apart from CD [EL3]. A comprehensive evaluation of clinical, radiological, 
histological, and microbiological evaluation helps discriminate IBD mimics from IBD [EL3].
Supporting text
Although IBD has no specific features, histology is an important parameter for diagnosis. The histological 
assessment of IBD is based predominantly on disease activity [epithelial damage, neutrophilic infiltration 
of epithelium, and crypts] and changes of chronicity [architectural distortion of crypts, crypt loss]. 
Various scores used in both UC and CD typically consider these two parameters along with some 
additional parameters [such as granuloma in CD, eosinophils, or lamina propria infiltrate].(149) 
However, none of these parameters is specific to IBD.
Several conditions can mimic IBD clinically [presenting with chronic diarrhoea] and histologically. 
The presence of non-caseating granulomas favours the diagnosis of CD. However, it is important to 
recognize that there are many differential diagnoses of granulomas.(150, 151) (152-156) Granulomas 
can occur in CD and infectious colitis [GITB, Schistosoma, Yersinia, Paracoccidioides, histoplasmosis]. The 
frequency of granulomas reported in CD is variable; granulomas are more likely to be detected in 
resection specimens than in endoscopic biopsies.(150) Furthermore, granulomas may be less common in 
delayed-onset CD.(157) Fissuring ulcers and focal mucosal disease are suggestive of CD.(158) GITB, 
which can mimic CD, can also present with granulomas. While caseating granulomas are specific to GITB, 
epithelioid granulomas can be found in both conditions.(154) Large granulomas [> 400 µm], confluent or 
coalescing granulomas, and a higher frequency of granulomas favours GITB.(153, 154, 159-162) Ulcers 
lined by a band of epithelioid histiocytes also suggest GITB.(160) Other features that may suggest GITB 
are granulomas in the caecum and at multiple sites.(160) Small granulomas, focally enhanced colitis, 
pericryptal granuloma, and deep ulcers suggest CD diagnosis over GITB.(160) Schistosomal colitis can 
cause epithelioid granulomas, with excess mucus, nearly normal crypts [occasionally crypt distortion], 
eosinophilic infiltration of the mucosa and submucosa, and occasionally the presence of Paneth cell 
metaplasia. Sometimes, calcified or uncalcified ova may be visualized in mucosal biopsies.(163, 164)  
Histoplasmosis can mimic IBD as it can cause an intestinal mass or ulcer-like lesions and histology may 
reveal granulomas and histoplasma-invaded macrophages. Special stains, such as silver stain, best

24
IBD IN LMICS
demonstrate histoplasmosis.(165, 166) Intestinal paracoccidioidomycosis may be an important mimic of 
IBD as it can present with abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and weight loss. Colonoscopy may reveal ulcers 
and strictures while histology may reveal granulomas. The presence of fungus appearing like a ship’s 
pilot wheel, best highlighted on Grocott-Gomori staining, is diagnostic.(167) Yersinia infection can also 
present with abdominal pain and diarrhoea, and the lesions can mimic CD with deep ulcers and 
cobblestoning. Histology may also show granulomas and inflammatory infiltrates. Yersinia diagnosis is 
usually based on a positive culture from stool or biopsy.(155) Gastrointestinal basidiobolomycosis is 
another infectious granulomatous colitis reported mainly in the Middle East and can present with pain, 
fever, and intestinal obstruction. Histology may show granulomatous inflammation with eosinophils and 
fungal elements.(168)
Certain non-infectious conditions can also mimic IBD. Behçet’s disease can also mimic IBD and can 
present with ulcers in the ileum and colon. However, the presence of oral or genital ulcers alerts 
clinicians to the diagnosis.(169) The ulcers can be irregular and punched out, and histology can reveal 
inflammatory infiltrates and may show fibrinoid necrosis.(170) Microscopic colitis is an important cause 
of watery diarrhoea with macroscopically normal mucosa. However, histology may show increased 
intraepithelial lymphocytes [lymphocytic colitis] or thickened bands of subepithelial collagen 
[collagenous colitis] without any distortion of crypt architecture. Biopsies should be obtained from 
different colon regions [right, transverse, and left colon], even if the mucosa appears normal.(171-175) 
Eosinophilic gastroenteritis [EGE] can also present with abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Extremely high 
levels of eosinophils in tissue biopsies suggest the diagnosis. Eosinophils are typically more prevalent in 
the right colon than in the left, and their distribution can vary by geographic region, seasons of the year 
making it difficult to establish a definitive cutoff for diagnosing EGE. Eosinophilic-dominant inflammation 
may also occur in IBD. Still, eosinophil numbers in IBD are lower than in other causes, such as EGE or 
parasitic infestations [Trichuris, Strongyloidiasis, Angiostrongylus], drug reactions [e.g. DRESS], or 
autoimmune diseases.(176) Eosinophils in the histology of patients with IBD may indicate disease 
activity.(177)
Patients with common variable immunodeficiency can also present with chronic diarrhoea; many of 
these patients have infectious aetiology like Giardiasis, while others may have villous atrophy or 
microscopic colitis-like changes.(178)

IBD IN LMICS
Statement 11a 
In TB-endemic areas, misdiagnosis can be the most important single factor in diagnostic delay of CD 
[EL3]. Diagnostic delay is more common in LMICs and is associated with low awareness and limited 
resources, leading to a more complicated disease course [EL3].
Supporting text
The time to diagnose IBD in LMICs is greater than in HICs, which may be due to differences in healthcare 
provision. In addition, the difficulty in differentiating between IBD and more prevalent infectious 
diseases is a barrier to IBD diagnosis in LMICs.(179) 
A further reported barrier to accurate diagnosis of IBD in LMICs is the perceived rarity of IBD and the 
consequent lack of clinical awareness, leading to a lower index of suspicion.(179) Data from a 
multicentre study in Korea revealed that the mean interval of diagnostic delay was 16.0 ± 33.1 months; 
however, this gap decreased significantly over time.(180) In many resource-limited countries, several 
cultural barriers exist for colonoscopy. Disease is usually ignored for a long time by patients before they 
seek medical attention due to the social stigma of chronic disease; accordingly, diagnosis is delayed.(10, 
181)
Diagnostic delay in CD is associated with significantly higher stenotic complications and the need for 
surgery. (181) Diagnostic delay in LMICs is usually multifactorial and related to inadequate disease 
awareness and poor access to and low availability of diagnostic services. A study from India revealed 
that up to a third of patients with CD are misdiagnosed as having GITB, and empirical anti-tuberculosis 
treatment [ATT] is the single most significant contributor to diagnostic delay in TB-endemic areas.(181) 
Extreme delay in CD diagnosis can occur due to the assumption of a GITB diagnosis and improvement of 
stricturing disease with surgery. These patients likely present with complicated phenotypes of 
postoperative CD.(182) Increasing disease awareness and improving local gastroenterology care by 
collaborations between gastroenterologists from LMICs can accelerate development of comprehensive 
gastrointestinal care. The availability of and maintenance service for endoscopy equipment are major 
challenges. Collaboration between the local community and non-profit organizations can be a 
sustainable source of continual funding for gastrointestinal units.(182, 183)

26
IBD IN LMICS
Statement 11b
Differentiating between GITB and CD is a complex challenge due to overlapping clinical, radiological, 
and histopathological features. A multifaceted approach using clinical, immunological, molecular, 
endoscopic, radiological, histopathological, and microbiological assessments is essential to improve 
diagnostic accuracy [EL3].
Statement 11c
Screening for active or latent TB before starting an anti-TNF agent, other biologic therapies, or a small 
molecule [immunosuppression] is essential in LMICs due to the high prevalence of TB in these regions. 
These therapies can significantly increase the risk of reactivating latent TB, which can lead to severe 
complications. Although tests such interferon-gamma release assays and tuberculin skin test have 
limitations, these tests play a crucial role in detecting latent TB, with interferon-gamma release assays 
offering more specificity in TB-endemic areas [EL2]. 
Statement 11d
In TB-endemic regions where diagnostic uncertainty between GITB and CD exists after colonoscopy, 
histology, microbiology, and a trial of ATT followed by a repeat colonoscopy after 2 months to assess 
response may be considered [EL3].
Supporting text
Screening for active or latent TB before starting treatment is essential. There is currently no data to 
determine the utility of TB screening prior to initiation of corticosteroids. Distinguishing GITB from CD is 
a significant clinical challenge due to the similarities between the two conditions and the high 
prevalence of TB in LMICs. GITB and CD are both chronic granulomatous diseases with overlapping 
symptomatology and radiological, endoscopic, and histopathological findings.  (22)A South Korean study 
revealed that approximately 11% of patients with GITB were misdiagnosed as having CD, while 
approximately 18% of patients with CD were misdiagnosed with GITB.(184) Misdiagnosis and delayed 
treatment can have serious consequences with a risk of disease progression, and starting ATT in patients 
with CD can have serious adverse effects. Furthermore, TB dissemination may occur in patients receiving 
immunosuppressive treatment who have GITB misdiagnosed as CD.(185) Making an accurate diagnosis 
as early as possible is essential to prevent complications. Differentiating between GITB and CD requires 
a multifaceted approach using detailed history [travel history, recent antibiotic use, exposure to TB] and 
a comprehensive clinical evaluation [including a chest X-ray] combined with an array of auxiliary tests

Page 317 / 432

27
IBD IN LMICS
that includes immunological, molecular, endoscopic, radiological, histopathological, and microbiological 
assessments, which are needed to improve diagnostic accuracy. Nevertheless, there is no single test that 
can clearly differentiate between GITB and CD. 
The sensitivity and specificity of the tuberculin skin test [TST] for GITB diagnosis is only 64.7% [95% CI: 
46.5–80.2%] and 73.3% [95% CI: 54.1–87.7%], respectively; the positive predictive value [PPV] is 73.3% 
[95% CI: 59.1–83.9%] and the negative predictive value [NPV] is 64.71% [95% CI: 52.6–75.2%]. 
Moreover, false-positive TST reactions can occur due to previous BCG vaccinations or other non-
tubercular mycobacterial infections, and false-negative tests can be seen after immunosuppressive 
therapy.(186) 
Although interferon-gamma release assays [IGRA] are relatively more specific for Mycobacterium 
tuberculosis, these are not always available in LMICs. A systematic review with a meta-analysis of IGRA 
accuracy showed that the mean sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV in the eight studies reviewed were 
81%, 85%, 78%, and 87%, respectively. In areas with a high TB incidence where latent infection is 
widespread, a positive QuantiFERON test result does not distinguish between active and latent TB. A 
retrospective study from China attempted to differentiate between GITB and CD based on the cutoff 
value of TB-IGRA. This study revealed that a TB-IGRA level > 100 pg/ml had a sensitivity of 88% and 
specificity of 74% for diagnosing GITB, and those with TB-IGRA level > 400 pg/ml had a more severe form 
of GITB.(187) However, this test is still necessary to exclude latent TB in IBD in TB-endemic regions, as 
evidenced by a high NPV [94.2%].(188) 
PCR analysis may help differentiate TB from CD by detecting M. tuberculosis DNA in biopsy or stool 
specimens. A systematic review and meta-analysis sought to determine the diagnostic accuracy of 
Xpert® MTB/RIF and revealed that it has modest sensitivity and reasonable specificity for diagnosing 
GITB.(189) Multiple attempts have been made to combine various features in models for distinguishing 
GITB and CD. In a landmark study by Limsrivilai et al., the investigators used a Bayesian model that 
demonstrated that the combination of gender, specific clinical manifestations, endoscopic features, and 
laboratory findings can accurately diagnose GITB in 91.8% of patients, with a sensitivity of 90.9% and a 
specificity of 92.6% for diagnosing GITB.(190) 
Finally, in TB-endemic regions where there is diagnostic uncertainty between GITB and CD [after 
colonoscopy, histology, and microbiology], a diagnostic trial of ATT should be considered, as treating 
GITB misdiagnosed as CD with immunosuppression may be detrimental.(185, 191) In these cases, 
mucosal healing should be assessed after 2 months of ATT, as lack of mucosal healing could suggest an

alternative diagnosis [including drug-resistant TB or CD].(192, 193) Asia-Pacific guidelines from 2016 
recommend 8–12 weeks of empirical ATT for patients with diagnostic uncertainty followed by a repeat 
colonoscopy and biopsy to assess response and to decide on the appropriate strategy.(88) In addition, 
declining levels of C-reactive protein [CRP] and FC may be predictive of a positive response to ATT. If 
levels remain elevated, this may suggest drug-resistant TB or CD.(194, 195) 
Medical treatment of IBD in LMICs
Statement 12
The increased risk of TB reactivation in IBD patients treated with steroids and advanced therapies is of 
particular concern in LMIC settings [EL2]. 
Supporting text 
Ileocolonic resection could be considered a viable first-line treatment for CD, particularly in areas with a 
high prevalence of TB.(196) In clinical practice, approximately 1–3% of IBD patients treated with anti-
TNF agents develop active TB even after a negative screening test. These findings have been confirmed 
by reports from several Asian countries, including India, Japan, and South Korea. (197) The risk is notably 
higher with anti-TNF agents when compared with other treatments, such as 5-ASA, corticosteroids, and 
immunomodulators. 
Overall, there have been very few reported cases of TB associated with vedolizumab clinical trials have 
reported only a few cases and post-marketing data also indicate a low risk, with seven cases of TB in 
over 110 000 patient-years of exposure. There are no available data comparing the TB risk between anti-
TNF agents and anti-integrin agents. Data on TB risk with newer biologics remain limited.(197) Data 
from non-IBD indications also suggest that ustekinumab has a low risk of TB. (197) Data from non-IBD 
indications suggest an increased risk of TB with tofacitinib, especially in TB-endemic regions.(198) 
Distinguishing GITB from CD remains challenging in India, with 10–15% of cases misdiagnosed. 
Historically, patients were treated with ATT for years before a CD diagnosis. Although improved 
diagnostics now allow for more accurate differentiation, nearly a third of patients with CD still receive 
ATT before a correct diagnosis. TB is confirmed if endoscopic and histological improvements are 
observed after ATT, while poor response may indicate CD or drug-resistant TB.(199)

29
IBD IN LMICS
HIV significantly increases TB risk in sub-Saharan Africa. A recent meta-analysis showed higher TB 
incidence among HIV-positive individuals than World Health Organization regional estimates. HIV status 
should be carefully evaluated in LMICs.(200)
Statement 13
Vaccinations against infectious diseases should be administered, preferably prior to starting
immunosuppressive therapy in LMICs [EL3]. However, the cost of vaccines, their availability, and local 
infection prevalence are particular considerations in LMICs [EL5].
Supporting text
In India, the vaccination rate of common vaccines [BCG, hepatitis B, diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis, oral
polio vaccine, and measles] is 81% and coverage with elective vaccines is lower.(201) A study from India 
revealed that only 9.2% of patients with IBD had completed the hepatitis B vaccine.(202) This suggests 
that vaccination in adult IBD patients is low in LMICs, such as India.(203)
There are only limited data in the literature to estimate the risk of infections in patients with IBD in 
LMICs. A study of 908 patients in north India revealed HBsAg positive status in 2.6%, 2.4% and 3% of 
patients with IBD, UC, and CD, respectively.(204) A region-specific meta-analysis found that the 
prevalence of HBsAg positive status in patients with IBD in India is 2.4% [range 1.4–3.4%].(205) Median 
anti-HBs titres were lower in patients with UC than controls [67 IU/L vs 105 IU/L; p < 0.01].(206) 
Adequate and effective immune responses were significantly lower in patients with UC than controls 
[82% vs 96%,
p = 0.001; 41% vs 66%, p < 0.001]. Multivariate analysis revealed that independent predictors of 
adequate immune response were non-exposure to therapies like steroids, thiopurines, and biologicals, 
male gender, and early disease [disease duration < 5 years]. This suggests that all patients with new 
diagnoses of IBD in LMICs should be offered vaccination against hepatitis B.
A study from India on 41 patients with CD revealed that all patients were positive for anti-hepatitis A 
antibodies, with similar titres as in controls.(207) A meta-analysis in patients with IBD showed that the 
rates of hepatitis A seropositivity were the highest in Asia.(208) Nearly a third of the patients had 
antibodies to SARS-CoV-2 in non-vaccinated patients with IBD from northern India.(209) This suggests 
that although vaccination is warranted against COVID-19, a subset of patients from India are already 
exposed to SARS-CoV-2. In Iran, rates of measles vaccination were 72.5% and 73.0% for patients with CD

IBD IN LMICS
or UC, respectively.(210) It has been suggested that all patients with UC aged > 50 years should receive 
the two-dose standard vaccination regimen for herpes zoster prior to use of advanced therapies, such as 
anti-TNF agents.(211) Except for live vaccines, immunization against preventable infections can be safely 
administered in patients with IBD, even those on immunosuppressive or biologic agents.(212)
A paediatric guideline from Asia suggested that all children with IBD should receive age-appropriate
vaccines according to the corresponding national vaccination program.(213) Wherever feasible,
live-attenuated vaccines should be given before initiation of immunosuppressive therapy in non-
immune patients. A delay in treatment to complete the vaccination schedule is not recommended in 
patients requiring urgent immunosuppressive treatment. This is consistent with recommendations for 
adult patients, where live vaccines should be given at least 4 weeks prior to starting immunosuppressive 
therapy and should be avoided 1–3 months after cessation of highly immunosuppressive therapies.(203)
Serological response to vaccination should be verified whenever possible after vaccination in
patients with IBD, especially those on biological therapy. Serological response to hepatitis B
vaccination should be verified 1–2 months after vaccination, and revaccination is
recommended in patients who do not achieve adequate response after a completed vaccine 
course.(214) Drugs such as immunomodulators and anti-TNF agents are associated with poor immune 
responses to hepatitis B vaccination.(215) As loss of protection can occur in the long term, monitoring of 
anti-HBs titres should be performed preferably every 2 years. A booster dose is recommended to restore 
anti-HBs titres > 100 mIU/mL, especially in patients undergoing treatment with anti-TNF agents.(214) A 
prospective study conducted in India revealed that all included patients with IBD had a serologic 
response to two doses of the COVID-19 vaccine (BNT162b2) that was similar to the response observed in 
the general population.(216)
Statement 14
The evidence for the use of empiric antibiotics during IBD flares in LMICs is currently not available 
[EL5].
Supporting text
In a study from Iran, C. difficile and Staphylococcus aureus were found in the intestinal microbiota of 
5.7% and 15.8% of patients with IBD flares, respectively.(217) Patients with UC who have intestinal 
colonization with extended-spectrum β-lactamase producers had more severe disease.(218)

31
IBD IN LMICS
Self-medication with antibiotics may delay the diagnosis of IBD.(10) Along with medical therapy, 
antibiotics were used in 45% of patients with IBD in a study in Iran.(219) The most commonly used 
regimen was a combination of metronidazole with ciprofloxacin. In patients from north India, antibiotic 
exposure [especially ofloxacin] was associated with disease flares.(220) A study from Pakistan revealed 
that 80% of IBD patients were taking antibiotics intermittently.(221) Empirical intravenous antibiotics 
[amoxicillin-clavulanate or piperacillin-tazobactam] were suggested as supportive treatment for children 
in LMICs presenting with acute severe UC with megacolon.(222) In a randomized controlled trial from 
India of adult patients with acute severe UC, adjuvant antibiotics [intravenous ceftriaxone and 
metronidazole] did not improve outcomes.(223) A meta-analysis of 13 trials on the use of antibiotics in 
UC did not change outcomes or need for second-line therapy or colectomy.(224) Although there may be 
a short-term benefit of oral antibiotics in non-severe active UC, the available studies are heterogeneous 
in terms of design and antibiotic regimen. The use of empirical antibiotics during IBD flares may be 
considered when the disease picture is not fully clear.
Statement 15
Access to modalities to monitor therapeutic response in IBD is suboptimal in LMIC settings [EL3]. 
Adoption of non-invasive modalities, such as routine tests, faecal biomarkers, and intestinal ultrasound 
are suggested [EL3]. 
Supporting Text
Clinical disease activity scores are useful for predicting remission, but are inadequate for therapy 
escalation due to poor correlation with intestinal inflammation or mucosal healing in CD. (213) 
Endoscopic assessment may be necessary if escalation is considered. In resource-limited settings, 
serial assessment of paediatric ulcerative colitis activity index [for paediatric UC] or weighted 
paediatric Crohn’s disease activity index [for paediatric CD], combined with biomarkers such as 
FC, can help prioritize patients for endoscopic evaluation.(225) In children with CD, elevated CRP 
[> 0.8 mg/dL] correlates with mucosal inflammation and active disease.(226) In paediatric UC, 
CRP correlates with colonoscopic inflammation, but normal CRP and erythrocyte sedimentation 
rate may not exclude active disease in one-third of children with intestinal inflammation.(227)
In adult IBD, disease response is assessed using clinical indices [Crohn’s disease activity index, 
Harvey-Bradshaw index, Simple Clinical Colitis Activity Index (SCCAI)], CRP, FC, and

IBD IN LMICS
endoscopy.(228-230) FC is the easiest and least expensive non-invasive biomarker for prediction 
of endoscopic and histological activity or indices.(231) Mean platelet volume [MPV] is 
significantly lower in patients with active disease than those in remission. Thus, MPV may be used 
as a cost-effective and non-invasive biomarker for monitoring disease activity.(232) CRP 
correlates with markers of clinical, endoscopic, radiological, and cross-sectional activity in IBD, 
especially CD. Thus, CRP may be helpful during patient evaluation. Use of CRP depends on disease 
presentation; although biological markers of inflammation should not be used for proctitis, CRP 
should be used as a measure of treatment efficacy for left-sided colitis and pancolitis.(233) The 
overall sensitivity for active enteric inflammation using capsule endoscopy [CE] versus magnetic 
resonance enterography [MRE], ileocolonoscopy [IC], or both was 94% versus 100% [ p = 0.125] 
and specificity was 74% versus 22% [ p = 0.001].(234) The sensitivity of CE was superior to MRE 
for enteric inflammation in the proximal small bowel [97% vs 71%; p = 0.021] and was similar to 
MRE, IC, or both in the terminal ileum and colon [ p = 0.500–0.625].(234) In a multicentre, 
prospective study that compared panenteric CE versus IC plus MRE in 158 patients with CD, there 
were seven serious adverse events, of which three were related to the CE device.(234) A study 
from India on 508 patients showed that colonoscopy could be avoided in 51.4% of patients with 
CD and 51.8% of patients with UC using intestinal ultrasound [IUS].(235) The study also showed 
that with IUS, cross-sectional imaging could be avoided in 38.1% of patients with suspected active 
small-bowel CD. IUS can also locate lesions in the ileum and colon in patients with CD.(236)
Statement 16
Complementary and alternative medicine [CAM] for IBD treatment is frequently used in LMIC settings 
[EL4]. Further evidence is required to characterize the efficacy and safety of CAM for IBD treatment 
[EL4].
Supporting Text
Traditional Chinese medicine [TCM] is one of the most developed and diversified complementary and 
alternative medicine [CAM] approaches. Chinese herbal medicine [CHM] administered orally or by 
retention enema has shown promise in managing IBD through various compounds that exhibit anti-
inflammatory effects in preclinical and clinical studies. Mode of therapy, combining both systemic and

IBD IN LMICS
local administration of TCM herbal drugs, should be considered.(237) CHMs contain active ingredients, 
such as polysaccharides, polyphenols, alkaloids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, can regulate gut microbiota, 
and can treat UC by repairing the intestinal epithelial barrier, modulating the immune system, and 
regulating metabolism.(238)
Studies from India (239, 240) showed that curcumin, a bioactive component of Curcuma longa, interacts 
with many cellular targets of inflammation, such as NF-κB, JAKs/STATs, MAPKs, TNF-α, IL-6, PPAR, and 
TRPV1. Curcumin significantly reduced the relapse rate of patients with quiescent IBD, suggesting that 
curcumin may be a CAM treatment for IBD. One randomized clinical trial from Iran reported the 
effectiveness of CAM using pomegranate peel in patients with UC. The study showed that the herb extract 
resulted in an increase in clinical response, more than two times over placebo (relative risk of clinical 
response was (2.27 [CI 95%: 0.97 to 5.29]). However, further studies in a larger sample of patients are 
warranted.(241) Polyphenols from food byproducts may be another possible CAM approach for IBD 
treatment, although further research is needed to understand their bioavailability, mechanisms of action, 
and safety to fully harness their therapeutic potential.(242)
Herbal bioactive components, such as curcumin, resveratrol, ellagic acid, silybin, catechin, kaempferol, 
icariin, glycyrrhizin, thymol, lupeol, rutin, hesperetin, gallic acid, boswellic acid, and quercetin, have been 
used in the treatment of IBD.(240)
A systematic review and meta-analysis found that indigo naturalis [Qing dai] might be effective in treating 
UC, with a clinical response rate of approximately 80% and a remission rate of around 67%. Most adverse 
effects were mild, except for one case of reversible pulmonary arterial hypertension. Despite concerns 
about study biases and generalizability, evidence suggests that indigo naturalis is a promising therapy for 
UC and warrants further research to compare its efficacy with conventional treatments.(243)
Coconut water is anti-inflammatory, can modulate the gut microbiome, and is a rich source of potassium. 
A single-centre placebo-controlled study on coconut water revealed that, when compared with placebo, 
a greater proportion of patients [121 screened patients, 95  included in modified intention-to-treat 
analysis] receiving coconut water achieved clinical response [57.1% vs 28.3%, OR: 3.4] and clinical 
remission [53.1% vs 28.3%; OR: 2.9] at 8 weeks. Gut microbiome changes were also assessed at the end 
of the study period which reported a reduction in Romboutsia timonensis, Escherichia, Streptococcus, 
Acidaminococcus, Parabacteroides, and Fusicatenibacter scatoligenes , and enhancement in Bacteroides 
ovatus, Prevotella copri, Holdemanella biformis, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Blautia, Catenibacterium 
mitsuokai, Ligilactobacillus ruminis, Eubacterium coprostanoligenes,  and Roseburia faecis  at 8

weeks.(244) Although CAM therapies are popular in China and India, they are not frequently used in some 
LMICs, such as countries in North Africa [Egypt, Tunisia, Algeria, Morocco](239, 245), indicating 
geographical differences in use of CAM therapies. Given the popularity of CAM therapies, further research 
is warranted to develop evidence-based guidance, especially considering the unique cultural preferences 
and resource constraints in LMICs.
Statement 17a
Dietary therapies are a valid component of IBD management in LMIC settings [EL2] where expertise 
and therapies are available [EL4]. 
Statement 17b
Malnutrition and its impact should be addressed while instituting dietary therapies [EL3].
Supporting text 
Special diets, such as exclusive enteral nutrition [EEN], specific carbohydrate diet, anti-inflammatory diet 
for IBD, and fermentable oligosaccharides, disaccharides, monosaccharides, and polyols [FODMAP], have 
positive effects on IBD management.(3) Dairy restriction is a controversial approach for patients. In a study 
on 1888 participants [IBD: n = 872, non-IBD: n = 1016] on the effect of dairy in IBD, dairy intolerance and 
lactase deficiency were more common in participants with IBD [65.5% vs 46.1%; p = 0.0001]. The rates of 
gastrointestinal complaints among participants with IBD who had no family history of lactase deficiency, 
history of food sensitivity, or both were 59.91% [ p = 0.68], 52.87% [ p = 0.98], and 50.33% [ p = 0.99] 
respectively, and were similar to participants without IBD.(246) 
EEN has been widely studied and is recommended as first-line therapy for inducing remission of luminal 
CD in paediatric population. EEN is a nutritionally complete liquid diet, either polymeric or oligomeric 
[semi-elemental or elemental], administered for 6–8 weeks through a nasogastric tube or orally.(247) The 
European Society for Pediatric Gastroenterology, Hepatology, and Nutrition recommends at least 6 weeks 
of EEN, even though the mucosa usually heals after 8 weeks. Studies from India have shown positive 
results for EEN for CD and acute severe UC.(248, 249)
One-third of IBD patients experience irritable bowel syndrome-like symptoms. A recent meta-analysis 
confirmed that a low-FODMAP diet reduced functional gastrointestinal symptoms, such as bloating. In 
healthy volunteers, the mechanisms of action behind a low-FODMAP diet include decreased 
Bifidobacterium and reduced breath hydrogen. (250)Elimination diets are not recommended and may 
lead to several nutritional deficiencies. Considering malnutrition in IBD patients, nutrition education by

35
IBD IN LMICS
trained dietitians is necessary for the patients to acquire disease-related knowledge and overall balanced 
nutrition as part of strategies in treating and preventing nutrition deficiencies. (251) Recently, a double-
blind clinical trial revealed that fructan composition and not gluten induced functional symptoms in 
patients with self-reported non-celiac gluten sensitivity. (252)Similar results were previously reported 
concerning lactose intolerance. (253) Avoidance of certain foods is a risk factor for malnutrition in IBD 
patients in a Spanish cohort.(254) Of note, malnutrition was associated with increased complications, such 
as higher surgery and hospitalization rates, and malnutrition is associated with reduced quality of life in 
IBD patients. Exclusion of dairy products, such as in a lactose-free diet or paleo diet, may increase 
osteoporosis risk in IBD patients, while gluten-free diets are associated with decreased microbiota 
diversity.(255) A Mediterranean diet [MD] may be beneficial in supporting medical therapy and influences 
disease course and quality of life in patients with IBD. The MD reduces inflammation and decreases the 
risk of other diseases [e.g. cardiovascular disease]. The cost of the MD is also lower than specific diets. 
Nevertheless, some foods in the MD may not be tolerated by patients with gastrointestinal disorders, 
including IBD. For this reason, tolerance to the MD should be considered before this diet is recommended 
to patients with IBD. The MD should be personalized for each patient with the aim of reducing symptoms 
and improving disease course.(256) 
Malnutrition is a particular concern for patients with IBD, especially for those with CD. 
Gastroenterologists should use malnutrition screening tools [e.g., Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool, 
Nutrition Risk Screening] for these patients. The assessment of nutritional status before dietary 
therapies may assist in developing personalized diets.(257, 258)
Statement 18
Non-adherence to medication is an underestimated problem in LMIC settings and has important 
consequences [EL3].
Supporting text
Poor adherence to medication is a major global issue. A World Health Organization report revealed that 
the average medication adherence rate among patients with chronic diseases worldwide is approximately 
50%. The report emphasizes that as the number of individuals with chronic diseases increases, the 
consequences of poor medication adherence become more severe. Non-adherence is a primary 
contributor to preventable morbidity, mortality, and increased healthcare costs.(259)

https://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/ecco-jcc

Page 326 / 432
========================================================================

36
IBD IN LMICS
A Lebanese survey study on physicians’ beliefs about medication adherence in immune-mediated 
inflammatory diseases revealed that most surveyed physicians [74%] did not systematically measure 
adherence in their practice, and 54% considered adherence as a treatment goal of equal or greater 
importance to therapeutic endpoints. Lack of time and specialized nursing support was reported as an 
important barrier to measuring adherence.(260) 
Whether patients living in LMICs are at greater risk for medication non-adherence is unknown. A study by 
Moshkovska et al . identified non-modifiable [younger age, South Asian ethnicity] and potentially 
modifiable [medication beliefs] predictors of non-adherence. Specifically, the relationship between 
perceived necessity and self-reported adherence was significant.(261) A recent Brazilian study revealed 
that medication non-adherence can be as high as 72.7% [CD: 68.1%, UC: 75.3%].(262) An study from India 
revealed that 81% of patients were non-adherent to treatment [defined as taking ≤ 80% of the dose 
advised]. The reasons for non-adherence were forgetfulness [77%], felt better [14.2%], high frequency of 
doses [10.1%], no effect of medications [7.87%], and non-availability of medications [2.3%]. Non-adherent 
patients were more likely to relapse when compared with those who were adherent [OR: 3.389, 95% CI: 
1.29–8.88; p = 0.012].(263) Conversely, a study from Iran reported high adherence rates [77.8%] and there 
was no statistically significant difference between adherence and non-adherence rates in terms of gender, 
educational status, disease type, disease severity, and family history of IBD.(264) Another study from India 
study showed that the rates of antibiotic use [29.4% vs 6.1%], lack of adherence [50.9% vs 15.2%], and 
CAM use [70.6% vs 33.3%] were higher in those experiencing a flare than in those not experiencing a flare. 
Patients attributed the lack of adherence to therapy cost, presumed cure [due to lack of symptoms], and 
fear of adverse effects.(220) 
Identifying patients with a higher likelihood of non-adherence is essential for both epidemiological studies 
and clinical practice, as this allows for targeted interventions. The frequent modifiable non-adherence 
risks were a poor understanding of treatment or disease, medication accessibility and an individual’s 
organisation and planning. Depression, negative treatment beliefs/mood and anxiety increased the non-
adherence likelihood. (265)
Statement 19
Access to advanced therapies is limited in LMIC settings and should be improved [EL3].
Supporting text

Page 327 / 432

37
IBD IN LMICS
Access to advanced therapies in sub-Saharan Africa is minimal, with marketing approval for certain 
therapies restricted to only a few countries. There is some availability on a case-by-case basis.(5) Access 
is mainly limited to anti-TNF agents.(5, 8) Although Africa accounts for 17% of the global population, it 
accounts for only 1% of monoclonal antibody sales.(266) Challenges in the use of advanced therapies 
are linked not only to approval but also to other factors, such as cost, cold-chain storage facilities, and 
transportation and manufacturing infrastructure.(5, 8)
Although the availability of more cost-effective biosimilars may address some of the cost burden, other 
factors must also be addressed.(267) The efficacy of adalimumab biosimilars has been shown in 
induction and maintenance in real-world studies in IBD.(268, 269) Further research is required to 
examine the barriers to access in LMICs and how these can be addressed. 
Statement 20a
There are unique considerations, such as availability and cost, that favour wide use of thiopurines in 
LMIC settings compared with HICs [EL3].
Statement 20b
Although frequently used in LMICs, there is no evidence to support use of 5-ASA for CD and should be 
avoided [EL3]. 
Supporting text
The MIRACLE trial revealed that thiopurines along with 5-ASA are an effective maintenance therapy for 
clinical remission after infliximab induction in steroid-refractory UC.(270) Corticosteroid-free sustained 
clinical remission rates were at 68% at 1 year, 59% at 2 years, and 42% at 4 years. A study from India on 
81 patients with fistulizing CD [most patients had complex fistulae] revealed a complete response rate of 
25% [IQR: 24–66] at 45 months of follow up after thiopurine monotherapy in a setting with limited 
biological availability.(271)
A study on 115 patients with stricturing CD revealed that thiopurine monotherapy has a good short-
term response with a modest long-term response.(272) The cumulative probability of maintaining 
response was 73% at 1 year, 40.7% at 2 years, and 18.5% at 5 years. A quarter of patients with 
stricturing CD developed leukopenia. Overall, thiopurines had a good sustained long-term response in a 
cohort of 988 patients with IBD.(273) Overall effectiveness of thiopurine at 5 and 10 years was 79% and 
72% in UC, and 69% and 63% in CD, respectively. A study of 1093 patients with IBD with 7 [range 4–12]

Page 328 / 432
========================================================================

38
IBD IN LMICS
years of follow-up revealed that long-term therapy with thiopurines is safe, with no reported cases of 
lymphoma or non-melanoma skin cancer.(274) 
Although there is limited information on use of thiopurines in sub-Saharan Africa, reviews from the 
rheumatology literature suggest that thiopurines are available in some countries in this region.(5) 
Thiopurines are the most common treatment after corticosteroids for management of CD in LMICs.(8) In 
low-resource countries, stopping aminosalicylates should be considered when there is an adequate 
response to thiopurines.(5, 8) Thiopurine metabolite testing is not available in many sub-Saharan 
countries, but an increase in Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) and a reduction in lymphocyte count may 
indicate that therapeutic levels have been achieved.(5) TPMT and NUDT15 testing is also limited in sub-
Saharan countries;  phenotyping assays may be an option for these countries. It is important to note 
that NUDT15 testing, if available, may be more relevant in some regions, including East and South 
Asia.(275, 276) Another approach to decrease the risk of bone-marrow suppression could be use of 
serial full blood counts to identify early bone-marrow suppression. An important factor to consider in 
regions with a high prevalence of HIV is the heightened risk of bone-marrow suppression in patients 
with IBD who are prescribed both thiopurines and the prophylactic HIV medication, co-trimoxazole.(8) 
As a result, thiopurines such as azathioprine and 6-mercaptopurine are commonly used in LMICs to treat 
IBD due to their affordability and availability compared with biologics. However, their use in these 
settings requires specific considerations, including the need for regular monitoring of blood counts and 
liver function to avoid potential toxicity, particularly given the challenges of limited healthcare 
resources. The higher prevalence of infectious diseases, such as TB and viral hepatitis in LMICs also 
necessitates caution, as thiopurines can suppress the immune system and increase the risk of infections. 
Cost-effectiveness, accessibility, and careful patient monitoring are essential factors in the effective use 
of thiopurines in these regions.
The Iranian Registry of Crohn’s and Colitis reported that the most common treatment for patients with 
CD in Iran was 5-ASA (84.8%), followed by immunomodulators (64.5%), Prednisolone (54.2%), and anti-
TNF agents (35.9%). The high prevalence of 5-ASA usage among patients with CD in this large cohort 
study indicates the need for clinician education.(277) 
Statement 21
IBD patients from LMICs are underrepresented in clinical trials of advanced therapies [EL1].

IBD IN LMICS
Supporting text
IBD is a global disease. Patients with IBD from South America and Africa are underrepresented in clinical 
trials of pharmacological agents. Only 9% and 5% of all trials on pharmacological agents in IBD were 
conducted in Africa or South America, respectively.(278) Of trials conducted in Asia, only 16% included 
patients with IBD from LMICs. 
Surgical management of IBD in LMICs
Statement 22 
In LMICs, a significant proportion of abdominal operations for IBD are performed in emergency 
settings, which may indicate delays in referral or economic constraints and may lead to poor 
outcomes [EL4].
Supporting text
A systematic review on the clinical characteristics of patients with UC in Asia revealed cumulative 
colectomy rates at 1 and 10 years of 2.4% and 7.6% in Hong Kong and 3.4% and 15.6% in Malaysia, 
respectively.(20) However, the study did not distinguish between colectomies performed as emergency 
or elective procedures.
A retrospective study from a Brazilian centre reported that 68.9% of surgical procedures on patients 
with CD were performed in an emergency setting [both abdominal and perianal procedures], with the 
most common indications being ileal strictures and anorectal fistulae. Although this study did not 
present differences in short-term results based on setting, it did show that emergency surgery was a 
predictor for surgical recurrence [Odds Ratio: 2.1, 95% CI: 1.6–6.6].(279)
A retrospective study from India assessed outcomes of 72 patients with UC who had emergency surgery 
and revealed an in-hospital postoperative mortality of 16.7%. The authors reported wound sepsis in 
37.5% of patients, fever in 27.8%, adhesive intestinal obstruction in 8.3%, pelvic sepsis in 6.6%, 
ileostomy-related complications in 6.6%, blow out of Hartmann’s pouch in 6.6%, and continued rectal 
bleeding in 3.3% of patients. The median duration of postoperative hospital stay was 10 days.(32) 
Another retrospective study from India presented data on 223 patients with CD of which 32.7% had at 
least one surgical procedure. Although the study did not distinguish between elective and emergency 
procedures, the analysis revealed male sex, small-bowel disease, perianal disease, and disease 
behaviour as independent predictors for surgery.(280)

40
IBD IN LMICS
A systematic review and meta-analysis revealed that emergency procedures are associated with poor 
outcomes in patients with CD undergoing bowel resections (281) and in patients with acute severe 
UC.(282) In LMIC settings, early surgical intervention in pre-operatively optimized patients might be 
associated with better outcomes, but there is a lack of evidence to support this hypothesis. 
Statement 23
The length of resected and remaining bowel segments in CD surgery is rarely reported in LMICs [EL5]. 
Structured and standardized reporting of the length of resected and remaining bowel segments during 
CD surgery in LMICs is a simple yet very useful improvement [EL4].
Supporting text
In LMICs, where healthcare resources are often limited, the precision of surgical interventions for IBD is 
paramount. Accurate measurement of resected and remaining bowel segments plays a crucial role in 
reducing the risk of disease recurrence, particularly in patients with CD,(283, 284) where inflammation 
can return at the site of anastomosis or other parts of the gastrointestinal tract.(285) 
Although the role of surgeon experience and training is critically important in LMICs, many surgeons 
may not have access to the same level of training and continuous professional development as their 
counterparts in HICs.(286) Resource limitations [e.g. lack of electronic documentation] may further 
negatively affect the surgeon’s performance [e.g. the meticulous measurements required during IBD 
surgery], leading to suboptimal outcomes.
A systematic review that included studies from HICs highlighted the critical nature of segmental 
resections in managing IBD-related colorectal cancer and dysplasia. The review found that inadequate 
resection margins, often due to the absence of advanced imaging and measurement tools, were 
associated with higher rates of metachronous cancer recurrence.(287) This underscores the importance 
of precise measurements during surgery to reduce risk of recurrence. A systematic review on surgery in 
CD concluded that an adequate report of resected small-bowel segments and length of remaining small 
bowel is of critical importance [e.g. for any planning of repetitive surgical interventions and subsequent 
management].(288) 
As there is no direct evidence from LMICs, recommendations must be carefully developed based on 
existing guidelines.

IBD IN LMICS
Statement 24
Surgical-site infections are the most frequent postoperative complications in LMICs [EL3]. The 
aetiology of these complications is multifactorial [e.g. pre-existing malnutrition, ignorance of disease 
behaviour, or delay in surgery] [EL3]. 
Supporting text
Postoperative complications following IBD surgery remain a significant concern in LMICs.(8, 22, 289) A 
retrospective study from Iran al. used records from six tertiary care hospitals to explore in the outcomes 
of adult patients with UC undergoing colectomy; of the 2933 admitted patients with UC, 4% needed 
colectomy and as many as 40% experienced postoperative complications, with bowel obstruction and 
pouchitis being the most common late complications.(290) 
Another retrospective study from a tertiary centre in China analysed the outcomes of 35 adult patients 
with IBD [CD: n = 21, UC: n = 14] undergoing laparoscopic surgery. The study reported a 26.3% morbidity 
rate for CD and 28.6% for UC, with complications such as wound infections [CD: 15.8%, UC: 16.7%] and 
intestinal obstructions [CD: 5.2%, UC: 8.3%].(291) 
Another study from China examined the prevalence and risk factors [e.g. male gender and disease 
behaviour] in the population studied. The authors recommended key strategies in managing 
complications [e.g. improving preoperative nutritional status and conservative surgical techniques].(292) 
A study from Brazil on 37 patients with CD (44 surgical procedures) revealed that 40.9% [18/44 ] of 
surgeries resulted in complications, including fistulae in 34.1% [15/44], abscesses in 22.7% [10/44], and 
anastomotic dehiscence in 11.3% [5/44]. Significant risk factors for these complications included longer 
disease duration [p = 0.04], penetrating disease behaviour [p = 0.013], extended surgical duration, 
[mean 181.1 minutes, p = 0.016], and larger resected specimens [median 25 cm, p = 0.014].(293) 
A paediatric study on early and long-term surgical outcomes and complications in a referral children’s 
hospital in Iran revealed that 21 paediatric IBD patients underwent a variety of surgical procedures, 
primarily due to anaemia, intestinal bleeding, or failure to thrive. A high rate of early postoperative 
complications was observed, including wound infections [23.8%] and pelvic abscesses [14.3%], with 
some cases requiring reoperation. The long-term complication rate was 28.6% during follow up. 
Predictors for long-term complications included lack of prior drug treatment and bleeding as surgical 
indications.(294)

Page 332 / 432

42
IBD IN LMICS
Potential complications in the context of ostomy creation were examined in a retrospective study 
conducted in Sri Lanka. The complication rate was high [34.2%] and included prolapse [16.4%], skin 
excoriation [15.1%], and parastomal hernia [9.6%]. In this context, the role of perioperative care by 
specialized enteral stoma therapists in reducing complication rates was emphasized, based on the 
experience with a lack of specialized care described in LMIC settings.(295) 
An older study from Pakistan described postoperative complications over a comparatively long period 
[1986 -2010]. The study examined a cohort of 36 adult patients with IBD [UC: 72%, CD: 28%], with most 
of them having a relatively low BMI. Importantly, up to 72% experienced postoperative complications, 
with wound infection as the most common early complication [30.5%], while late complications included 
pouchitis in those who underwent IPAA [44.9%] and strictures [5.5%]. The study also reported a 
mortality rate of 38.8%, which is considerably higher than the values reported internationally.(75)
Surgical outcomes in ileocecal CD were examined in a multicentre study from Latin America 
[SURGICROHN-LATAM], which analysed 337 patients from 10 tertiary referral centres. Patients were 
stratified into Early CD [ECD, n = 60] and Complicated CD [CCD, n = 277] groups. CCD patients 
experienced more postoperative complications [33.21% vs 16.67%], longer operating times [median 
164.25 vs 90.53 minutes], and higher reoperation rates [13.36% vs 3.33%]. Smoking was more common 
in the CCD group than in the ECD group [22.38% vs 8.33%, p = 0.013]. The study also identified greater 
operating time, additional associated procedures, and intraoperative complications as significant risk 
factors that independently contribute to increased postoperative morbidity, particularly in those with 
complicated disease. This study demonstrated that earlier surgical intervention in luminal disease 
significantly reduces postoperative morbidity compared with delayed surgery in complicated cases in 
LMICs.(296) 
A retrospective analysis of surgical outcomes of 397 patients with IBD [CD, n = 136; UC, n = 261] in a 
tertiary colorectal unit in South Africa over a 14-year period [2005–2018] revealed a higher 
postoperative mortality rate in patients with UC [17.7% vs 3.5%]. The most frequent postoperative 
complications were surgical-site infections, anastomotic leaks, and fistula formation.(297) The risk of 
post-operative complications in patients with IBD who underwent surgical resection was investigated in 
studies from Latin America (298) and in a crowd-science based review with participation of surgeons and 
gastroenterologists from LMICs.(299)

IBD IN LMICS
Statement 25
Adoption of Enhanced Recovery After Surgery [ERAS] programs remain a challenge in LMICs [EL4]. 
Wider adoption of ERAS programs could significantly improve postoperative outcomes, especially in 
settings with limited resources and high postoperative morbidity rates [EL5].
Supporting text
Enhanced Recovery After Surgery [ERAS] programs are multimodal perioperative care pathways 
designed to minimize physiological disturbance, thus facilitating early recovery following major surgery. 
A systematic review of ERAS programs for IBD surgery revealed a benefit from consistent adoption of 
this pathway.(300) While direct evidence of implementing such programs in LMICs is still lacking, 
insights can be drawn from specific studies. For example, a study in India on surgical management of CD 
in a tertiary care centre over an approximate 8-year period analysed the outcomes of 28 patients [male 
= 18, mean age 31.2 years] who underwent surgical resection for small-bowel involvement. Patients 
presented with complications, such as subacute intestinal obstruction, fistulae, and perforations, which 
required either elective or emergency surgeries. Notably, preoperative conditions such as anaemia, 
malabsorption, and growth retardation, along with use of steroids and immunosuppressants, were 
identified as significant risk factors that adversely affected postoperative outcomes, leading to 
complications such as anastomotic leak.(301) 
While this study did not specifically evaluate ERAS protocols, it underscores the challenges faced in LMIC 
settings, particularly the high rates of postoperative morbidity and the lack of structured recovery 
protocols. The findings highlight the critical need for improved postoperative care and patient education 
in such settings. Investment in education and training is needed in LMICs to implement ERAS programs. 
This will be economically beneficial in LMICs, as it will improve postoperative outcomes and reduce 
costs.(302) 
Statement 26 
In refractory moderate-to-severe UC in LMICs, transitioning from medical to surgical management is 
indicated by non-response to intensive medical therapies. Early surgical intervention can improve 
outcomes and reduce mortality, especially in the presence of life-threatening complications such as 
colonic perforation, toxic megacolon, or severe bleeding [EL3]. Close collaboration between 
gastroenterologists and surgeons is essential to improve outcomes.
Page 44 of 94
https://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/ecco-jcc

Page 334 / 432
========================================================================

44
IBD IN LMICS
Supporting text
Early surgical intervention is crucial for minimizing complications and mortality in refractory severe UC, 
as access to advanced therapies and intensive care is limited in LMICs. Observational studies suggest 
that delayed surgical intervention correlates with increased morbidity and mortality, highlighting the 
necessity for timely and decisive surgical action. Early surgical assessment can prevent the emergence of 
severe complications as well as deterioration in patient quality of life. This was shown in a study from 
India on the experience of almost three decades at a tertiary care centre.(32) In this study, the 
outcomes of 72 patients with severe, refractory UC undergoing emergency surgery were examined. 
These patients, as a primary criterion for transitioning to surgery, had either failed intensive medical 
therapy [e.g. no improvement] or developed complications, such as severe bleeding, colonic 
perforation, or toxic megacolon. Surgical interventions varied from subtotal colectomy to ileostomy. 
This study revealed a critical finding, namely surgeries performed within 5 days of failed medical therapy 
significantly reduced mortality rates compared with later interventions. Outcomes improved over time, 
with mortality rates decreasing from 23.3% in the earlier years [1985–1995] to 6.9% [1996–2003], which 
was attributed to better preoperative care and strategic surgical timing. This retrospective analysis 
highlights the importance of timely, coordinated care between physicians and surgeons to optimize 
outcomes. Importantly, patients who underwent surgery within 5 days of failed intensive medical 
therapy had significantly lower mortality rates [0%] than those who had delayed surgery beyond 5 days 
[23.5%]. The study also stressed that poor nutritional status contributes to higher morbidity and 
mortality rates. 
Transitioning from medical to surgical IBD management was also explored in a comprehensive 
investigation from Asia, e.g. the Asia-Pacific Crohn’s and Colitis Epidemiology Study [ACCESS], covering 
China, Indonesia, Sri Lanka, Macau, Malaysia, and Thailand, in which 413 IBD patients were evaluated in 
detail.(303)
Another large study exploring the epidemiology and treatment challenges of UC in Africa and the Middle 
East (304) revealed the transition to surgical management to specifically occur when there was failure of 
corticosteroids and immunomodulators for induction or maintenance of remission. Beyond the 
documentation of specific figures from LMICs such as Senegal or Burkina Faso, a notable finding of this 
review was the 65% increase in UC incidence in Ghana and Tunisia over the past two decades, again 
highlighting the challenges of managing UC in these regions. In fact, the study specifically emphasizes

45
IBD IN LMICS
that in these LMICs, the decision to perform surgery is often made when the patient’s quality of life has 
significantly deteriorated due to the disease and when medical treatments have failed to provide relief. 
The lack of timely surgical intervention was again shown to further exacerbate the disease burden, 
leading to poorer outcomes.
Based on the analysed studies, it is recommended that early surgery is prioritized in cases of severe UC 
when medical treatments fail, particularly within 5 days, to improve survival rates and patient 
outcomes. This recommendation is critical in LMICs, where delays can lead to significantly poorer 
outcomes.
Statement 27 
In LMICs, physical examination as a standalone diagnostic tool is commonly practiced to evaluate 
perianal CD. Endoanal ultrasound is suggested as a less expensive alternative to pelvic magnetic 
resonance imaging, provided there is sufficient availability and specific expertise [EL5].
Supporting text
Of the few studies on available imaging modalities for assessing perianal CD in LMICs, a multicentre 
audit from Colombia on 65 patients with perianal CD revealed physical examination as the most 
common method for the initial diagnosis [61.5%]. Pelvic magnetic resonance imaging [MRI] was 
performed in 27.7%, endorectal ultrasound [US] in 6.2%, and clinical examination under anaesthesia in 
only 1 patient.(305) Two additional studies, one describing clinical and demographic characteristics of 
IBD patients in an underdeveloped region in Brazil (306), and one reporting methods to differentiate 
intestinal TB from CD in India,(199) did not discuss the available imaging methods to assess perianal 
disease. A study from north India reported MRI features of 27 patients with perianal CD, focusing on 
clinical and radiological characteristics and patient management; MRI was suggested as a mandatory 
imaging modality for all patients with perianal CD.(307)
Although a study on perianal fistulizing CD in India notes that endoanal US is less expensive, this study 
did not provide any data on actual access to MRI or endoanal US in this setting.(308)
Pelvic MRI was the imaging modality used in a recent cross-sectional study from Brazil that evaluated the 
effect of infliximab serum concentrations on disease activity in perianal fistulizing CD,(309) which was 
consistent with the Brazilian Consensus Statement as of 2022. Based on a Delphi consensus considering 
expert and stakeholder opinions, pelvic MRI was recommended to evaluate fistulizing perianal CD,

https://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/ecco-jcc

whereas endoanal US could also be used, depending on availability and expertise.(310) However, this 
recommendation was based on studies and guidelines from HICs.
Statement 28 
In LMICs, sphincter-cutting techniques, such as fistulectomy, fistulotomy, and cutting seton are still 
widely used for surgical management of complex perianal CD. However, recent studies have shown 
good results from the adoption of sphincter-preserving techniques [EL4]. 
Supporting text
Several studies from LMICs examined the use of cutting seton and fistula plugs in anal fistula surgery.  
One study from a tertiary referral centre in Egypt described 7 patients undergoing anal surgery for CD 
without providing further detailed information.(311) More detail was provided in a relatively recent 
study from India (271) on 65 patients with CD, of whom 58% received at least one surgical procedure, 
namely fistulectomy or fistulotomy, followed by seton placement. An alternative approach, video-
assisted anal fistula treatment, was used for only 18% of these patients. A multicentre study from 
Colombia on 65 patients with perianal fistulizing CD revealed that 46% of these patients had a surgical 
procedure, namely mucosal flap surgery [18.5%], ileostomy [13.8%], ligation of the internal tract [6.2%], 
closure of the internal orifice [4.6%], temporary colostomy [4.6%], and definitive colostomy with 
proctectomy [4.6%].(305) 
A retrospective assessment from a tertiary referral centre in Brazil,(312) including 625 patients with IBD 
[416 with CD], also provided some information on patients with perianal CD who received relatively 
conventional procedures [e.g. fistulotomies and setons]. Recently, there have been a few reports on 
newer methods. An expert group from a tertiary referral centre in Malaysia used laser ablation of fistula 
tracts in 63 patients, of which 3 [4.3%] had IBD.(313) In a clinical interventional study at an IBD referral 
centre in Iran, 5 adult patients with refractory disease and draining perianal fistulae due to CD received 
mesenchymal stromal cells, injected locally and combined with fibrin glue, which was a potentially safe 
and effective therapeutic approach. (271)Lastly, a narrative overview on current IBD therapies made a 
specific point in identifying challenges for CD surgery in limited-resource settings in South Asia but did  
not address surgical management of perianal CD in detail.(308)

69
IBD IN LMICS
Table 1. Challenges and suggested solutions for policymakers
Challenges and solutions—Epidemiology
Challenges Solutions
Insufficient IBD data 
documentation
• Implement and develop IBD registries, prospective cohort studies, or both 
at an appropriate level [e.g. national, regional, hospital-based, population-
based, community-based], depending on the organization of health 
systems and healthcare services for patients with IBD 
• Implement IBD registries with appropriate data governance
• Develop training courses on high-quality data collection 
• Involve primary care in data collection 
• Support research initiatives for new data collection of potential risk 
factors
Insufficient awareness of 
IBD
Increase IBD awareness of [public] health authorities by:
• Regular reporting of IBD incidence and prevalence rates using data from 
implemented registries
• Information provided by medical societies, including information on 
challenges related to affordability of equitable access to healthcare and 
therapies for local communities, minorities, and economic impact
Cultural barriers for IBD 
patients
• Develop educational programs for the general population that consider 
the needs and expectations of local communities
• Engage community leaders and healthcare providers in culturally sensitive 
education programs to encourage early medical consultation and 
adherence to treatment plans
• Provide access to general information regardless of gender or ethnicity
Limited availability of 
specialized care
• Establish specialized training programs for healthcare professionals in 
gastroenterology, focusing on IBD management, to build capacity in LMICs
• Increase the number of training courses and programs to allow greater 
access for professionals, possibly through telemedicine where appropriate 
• Provide access to courses regardless of gender or ethnicity
Support for technical 
developments 
• Support initiatives for the development of registries or infrastructure for 
IBD biomedical data collection
• Support development initiatives for digital tools 
Challenges and solutions—IBD diagnosis 
Lack of experts in 
histopathology, advanced 
imaging, endoscopy, and 
bowel ultrasound 
• Initiate education programs as soon as possible  
• Provide grants for education [IBD training] and for research
• Provide grants for mobilization of personnel with expertise in the LMIC for 
6-8 weeks to train personnel and establish proper management 
protocols on-site 
• Telemedicine 
• Second-opinion platforms for imaging and histopathology
Lack of 
colonoscopy access 
• Government support and NGO grants to build endoscopy facilities 
• Education and training programs for colonoscopy providers
Limited accessibility to 
specific tests, such as FC, 
Quantiferon, IGRA, and 
rapid molecular tests, 
such as multiplex panels 
for GI infections 
• Government support and NGO grants

Page 360 / 432
========================================================================

70
IBD IN LMICS
Lack of access to 
advanced imaging 
modalities, such as MRI 
• Government support and NGO grants  
• Funding should consider maintenance and running costs, not just 
infrastructure establishment 
Difficulties in 
differentiating GITB from 
IBD in endemic regions 
• Collaborative research, high-end centres adopting IBD centres in LMICs   
Low endoscopic capacity 
in sub-Saharan Africa, 
especially in outlying 
rural areas 
• Allocated funds  
• Dedicated facilities 
• Accessibility and transportation to well-equipped centres 
Challenges and solutions—Medical treatment of IBD
Cost of therapy, 
especially with long-term 
treatment
• Equitable access to medications in LMICs, especially advanced therapies 
for managing IBD 
• Providing generic medications or biosimilars to patients should reduce the 
cost of therapy, and comprehensive health insurance may benefit patient 
care in IBD
Cost of diagnosis and 
follow up of IBD
• Routine use of FC as a monitoring test in IBD is restricted by cost, access, 
and availability; an inexpensive point-of-care test to assess flare or 
remission would impact patient care
• Intestinal ultrasound may be a good modality for monitoring response to 
therapy in IBD patients in LMICs. Treating clinicians should be trained and 
educated in intestinal ultrasound to make it a treat-to-target tool that is 
widely available in LMICs 
• In the paediatric population, small-bowel evaluation is challenging as 
intestinal ultrasound requires further study; however, the use of CE and 
MRE is not available widely in LMICs
• Standardized endoscopic reporting using validated endoscopic scores, 
such as the Mayo endoscopic score for UC and SES-CD for CD, is crucial for 
assessing mucosal inflammation and disease activity in IBD patients. 
Lack of specialized care 
and the absence of 
comprehensive 
treatment frameworks
• Implementation of a Multidisciplinary Team [MDT] approach is 
particularly crucial in these settings, where the burden of IBD is increasing 
yet access to specialized care remains scarce 
• An MDT model is essential to improve disease outcomes, reduce 
complications, and improve quality of life for patients in LMICs, where 
healthcare systems often struggle to provide the necessary level of 
coordinated care
• MDTs can facilitate implementation of standardized care pathways and 
optimize use of available resources, making it a vital strategy for 
improving IBD management in resource-limited settings
Challenges and solutions—Surgical treatment of IBD
A significant proportion 
of abdominal procedures 
for IBD in LMICs are 
performed in an 
emergency setting
• Creation of [accredited] IBD [tertiary] referral centres is key to improving 
IBD care. There should be an initial focus on country- or region-specific 
centralization
• Creation and promotion of MDTs to manage IBD will allow early referral 
to surgery in case of non-response to medical treatment
Cost of minimally 
invasive surgery [MIS]
• The cost of MIS must be considered in patients with IBD, as they are 
expected to have multiple surgeries. Therefore, regional and national 
collaboration for regular [hands-on] training and accreditation of IBD 
surgery, including MIS, should be promoted

IBD IN LMICS
• International collaboration should be promoted to facilitate capacity 
building 
Enhanced Recovery After 
Surgery [ERAS] programs 
are rarely implemented 
in LMICs
• Education of nursing staff on the principals of ERAS programs will improve 
outcomes without significant increases in healthcare costs; this 
investment will reduce complications
Surgical wound infections 
are the most frequent 
postoperative 
complication in LMICs
• Investments in improving hygiene
• Implementation of specialized IBD care units and centralization of surgical 
care for complex cases will reduce infectious complications
Limited use of endoanal 
ultrasound to assess 
perianal fistulae
• Provide education on use of endoanal ultrasound to assess perianal 
fistulae 
• Ultrasound is cheaper and more accessible compared with MRI

IBD IN LMICS
Table 2. Suggested research to address gaps in knowledge  
Gaps in knowledge—Epidemiology 
Gaps Suggested research 
Epidemiological data • Trends in national and regional incidence and prevalence of IBD in 
LMICs
• Identification of local risk factors for development and evolution of 
IBD
• Association studies of IBD and local risk factors
Role of genetic variants • Assessment of prevalence of specific genetic variants identified in 
populations from Southeast Asia, South Asia, and the Middle East, 
such as NOD2/CARD15 and ATG16L1 polymorphisms 
• Assessment of risk of IBD and genetic variants in LMIC populations
Dietary patterns • Risk of IBD and traditional versus Westernized dietary patterns in 
LMIC populations
• Role of ultra-processed food intake in the risk of developing IBD in 
LMICs
Environmental risk factors • Role of key environmental factors [e.g. rapid urbanization and 
urban living conditions, lifestyle habits including smoking, early 
exposure to environmental pollutants, domestic refrigeration] on 
IBD incidence
• Potential differences in environmental factors across regions, 
ethnic groups, and communities and their association with IBD 
incidence
Access to healthcare • Possible barriers to healthcare access and strategies for 
improvement
• Effect of restricted or delayed access to healthcare and therapies 
on disease course and main clinical and quality-of-life outcomes
Gaps in knowledge—Diagnosis of IBD
Infections, including 
gastrointestinal infections, that 
can mimic IBD flares 
• Molecular diagnostic panels in IBD flares in the context of LMICs  
Prevalence of different infection 
mimics in different LMICs by 
region 
• Prevalence of different infection mimics in different LMICs by 
region [e.g. GITB, amebiasis, other parasites, and specific bacterial 
infections] should be determined to guide diagnostic algorithms, 
practical approaches, and empiric therapies 
Real-world data on the impact 
of empiric antibiotic therapy 
• Collection of real-world data on the impact of empiric antibiotic 
therapy for patients with suspected IBD 
GITB   • Prospective multicentre studies to identify molecular tests for 
GITB with sufficient sensitivity 
Impact of infectious aetiology 
mimicking IBD in LMICs 
• Effect of infectious aetiology mimicking IBD in LMICs 
• Combination of FC with intestinal ultrasound and other ‘red flags’ 
to improve screening strategies based solely on symptoms to guide 
referrals for colonoscopy or gastroenterology consultations 
Role of sedation during 
endoscopy
• Characterizing patient preferences, satisfaction, and understanding 
of the procedure [endoscopy with or without sedation] 
Gaps in knowledge—Medical treatment of IBD

73
IBD IN LMICS
Patients from LMICs are not 
properly represented in drug 
trials
• Representation and inclusion of IBD patients from LMICs in drug 
trials 
• Specific populations of paediatric IBD, particularly very early onset 
IBD, should also be represented in future trials, as data from this 
paediatric population in LMICs are limited
Limited high-quality studies on 
empirical antibiotics in IBD 
flares, especially in patients 
with mild-to-moderate disease 
from LMICs
• High-quality studies on empirical antibiotics in IBD flares in patients 
with mild-to-moderate disease in LMICs
Limited high-quality studies on 
complementary and alternative 
medicine [CAM] for IBD 
treatment from LMICs 
• High-quality studies on CAM in IBD treatment in LMICs
• Efficacy and safety of CAM in IBD
• Efficacy of traditional therapies in combination with standard of 
care
Role of various diet and dietary 
components in the management 
of IBD in LMIC settings 
• Role of various diet and dietary components in the management of 
IBD in LMIC settings
• Dietary components that trigger flares or induce remission
Effectiveness of standard 
vaccinations
• Effectiveness of standard vaccinations and the need for boosters in 
patients with IBD on advanced therapies in LMICs
Gaps in knowledge—Surgical treatment of IBD
Outcomes of emergency versus 
elective IBD surgery
• Prospective observational studies on IBD surgery to compare 
surgical outcomes in emergency versus elective settings  
• Real-world results may identify specific risk factors for poor 
outcomes according to countries or regions and strategies to 
improve outcomes
Outcomes of minimally invasive 
surgery [MIS]
• Role of MIS and its impact on short- and long-term outcomes 
• The effect of implementation of national and regional IBD patient 
registry on surgical care 
Management of refractory 
moderate-to-severe UC in LMICs
• Management of refractory moderate-to-severe UC in LMICs to 
investigate the transition from medical to surgical care, the role of 
multidisciplinary teams, and outcomes
Enhanced Recovery After 
Surgery [ERAS] in LMICs
• Studies on ERAS programs in LMICs
Management of perianal 
fistulae and outcomes in LMICs
• Management of perianal fistulae and outcomes in LMICs to target 
areas of care that need improvement

Page 364 / 432
